findings.claim: ADRs 0040, 0041, 0042, and 0043 form a recurring-revision set: each parks, refines, or bounds the same unresolved primitive—how to build even, well-joined connect-mode kerning/spacing for difficult hands (the 'assembled-glyph feedback' discrimination problem)—without any of them closing it. 0040 parks the contextual kern milestone explicitly pending an assembled-glyph feedback pass; 0041 parks the dense-body kern and defers to a connection-point spec; 0042 bank thin-hand joins at ~B and defers Phase 3; 0043 proves a placement route but explicitly parks it again on the bridge-vs-weld discrimination problem.

citations.quote: PARK the milestone. Five build-time refinement approaches were each built and measured against the corpus; all five fail for ONE root reason
PARK the dense-body kern. Two implementations were built and measured; both fail
BANK thin-hand join evenness at ~B and defer Phase 3 to a focused milestone (the updated spec).
It is PARKED at one remaining problem: a placement-aware protection layer that tells a deliberate thin bridge from a body weld.

findings.consequence: A maintainer following the ADR chain would see four consecutive 'Accepted' records each claiming to make progress on connect-mode join/kern evenness, but none of them actually resolves the underlying question of how to distinguish an intentional connector bridge from a genuine weld at build time; the decision remains open across the whole set and should be tracked as a single still-open item rather than four closed decisions.

findings.claim: ADR 0033, 0040, 0041, and 0043 all circle the same unresolved primitive of build-time (vs assembled/rendered) measurement being insufficient to discriminate connect-mode seam defects from legitimate joins, with each record re-stating and re-parking the same 'no build-time measure separates X from Y' problem without resolving it (resolution only arrives later in ADR 0049-0052, which are not part of this set).

citations.quote: No build-time measure can tell that intentional overlap from a body weld, and the gap the eye actually reads does not exist until the glyphs are ASSEMBLED.
A build-time proxy diverges from the render — the same root as ADR 0040.
That discrimination is ADR 0040's assembled-glyph problem in its minimal, tractable form — the geometry is now known at placement time.

findings.consequence: If accepted, these three (or four counting 0033's framing) records should be read as one continuously deferred decision about assembled-vs-build-time measurement rather than as three independently closed decisions; downstream ADRs that build on 'ADR 0040 parked' status should be checked to confirm they don't assume it was resolved before ADR 0049.
